heading: DI TUTTE LE OPERE DANDO A TUTTI
card_summary: This chunk opens the sixth "tone" (Tono Sesto) of the Secondo Cantico of De Harmonia Mundi, under the heading "La facolta di operare" (the faculty of operating/working). Zorzi's central argument, drawn from Paul's Letter to the Hebrews and developed through Pseudo-Dionysius's Divine Names (citing Hierotheus), Origen, Plato's Parmenides and Epistles, and Kabbalistic sefirot, is that Christ is the author and perfecter of all faith and all creaturely operations. He then elaborates Christ's omnipresence as the metaphysical plenitudo of all things by identifying him with every element, metal, plant, animal, celestial body, and planet, as well as with artificial and divine constructs (tabernacle, house of God, the Hebrew letter Beth). The Trinitarian structure maps onto the Neoplatonic triad of One/Being/Good: the Father fills with unity, the Son with being and truth, the Spirit with goodness. The Kabbalistic "ten elements" of Hebrew theology are read as pointing to Christ as the Verbum incarnatum, invoking Baruch 3 and Dionysian thearchia.
essay:
# DI TUTTE LE OPERE DANDO A TUTTI — Chunk 79 (pp. 1739–1808)

**Note:** This summary covers the opening portion of a very long section (27,630 words). The analysis is based on the first approximately 500 lines of the text, which correspond to the introductory chapters of the Tono Sesto and its subsections up through Chapter V. The remaining pages, which continue the enumeration of Christ's names and the transition to the theme of "giving to all," are not covered here.

---

## The Faculty of Operating: Christ as Author and Perfecter of Faith

The chunk opens the sixth "tone" (Tono Sesto) of the Secondo Cantico under the sub-heading *La facoltà di operare* ("The Faculty of Operating"). Zorzi's first chapter argues that Christ is not merely an object of faith among others but the very author (*author*) and perfecter (*consummator*) of faith and of all creaturely operations. The argument is anchored in Paul's Letter to the Hebrews, which he quotes as the governing lemma:

> «Corriamo con perseveranza nella corsa che ci sta davanti, tenendo fisso lo sguardo su GESÙ, autore e perfezionatore della fede.»

<cite>p. 1571 (Italian text, lines 10–11)</cite>

Zorzi's move is characteristically synthetic: natural reason can demonstrate that God exists and created the world (he notes that even Aristotle, "awakened from the sleep of the senses," does not dissent from other sages on God as Artificer), but the fact that God created everything *through the incarnate Word* is knowable only from Scripture. This distinguishes properly theological knowledge from philosophical knowledge — a recurring methodological claim in De Harmonia Mundi.

He then passes through all twelve articles of the Apostles' Creed to show that each one refers to, emanates from, or leads back to Christ. The Creed becomes a demonstration of Christ's structural centrality to all believing and all acting:

> «Omnia credenda aut sunt circa Christum, aut emanant ab ipso, aut ad eundem referuntur. Facienda vero, et mandata omnia, quamvis extranea, et separata videantur ab eo nihilominus sine ipso conduci nequeunt, qui ait: Sine me nihil potestis facere.»

<cite>p. 1573 (Latin text, lines 38–40)</cite>

---

## Kabbalistic Convergence: The Ten Elements and Baruch 3

A pivotal moment occurs when Zorzi introduces "la teologia degli ebrei" and its "dieci elementi" (the ten sefirot) as a further witness. He cites Baruch 3:37 — *"Hic adinvenit omnem viam disciplinae, et tradidit eam Iahacob puero suo"* — as proof that the divine Wisdom transmitted in the Hebrew tradition was the same Logos that became incarnate as Jesus Christ. This is Zorzi's habitual Kabbalistic Christology: the sefirot of the Hebrew kabbalah find their fulfilment and explanation in the person of Christ.

---

## Trinity and the Neoplatonic Triad: Filling All Things

Chapter II, "Cristo, non solo per la fede, ma per tutte le cose, riempie tutto con la sua divinità," develops a Trinitarian ontology closely modelled on Proclean-Dionysian Neoplatonism. Citing Origen, Zorzi maps the three divine Persons onto three metaphysical donations: from the Father we receive *esse* (being as unity); from the Son, *intelligere* and ordered existence; from the Holy Spirit, sanctification and goodness.

He then aligns this with the ancient theologians' thesis that *the One* precedes being, which he assigns to the Father as *prima omnium origo*, while *esse* belongs to the Son as supreme Wisdom and is communicated through him to creatures. The Spirit fills all things with goodness, following the Psalmist (*"Aperiente te manum tuam omnia implebuntur bonitate"*).

The Neoplatonic transcendentals — One, Being, and Good — are explicitly equated with Father, Son, and Spirit:

> «Come l'Uno, l'Ente e il Bene sono la stessa cosa, come affermano quanti insegnano i principi della dialettica, così il Padre, il Figlio e lo Spirito santo sono una cosa sola quanto all'essenza, benché siano distinti nelle loro relazioni.»

<cite>p. 1577 (Italian text, lines 196–198)</cite>

The centrepiece of this section is an extended citation of Pseudo-Dionysius's *De divinis nominibus*, where Hierotheus's description of Jesus's deity is reproduced at length: Christ is *causa omnium et adimpletiva*, the recall and resurrection of the fallen, the illumination of the illuminated, the *thearchia* of those being deified, the simplicity of the simple, the unity of those tending to unity — and "la vita dei viventi, la sostanza degli esistenti, il principio e la causa di ogni vita e sostanza."

---

## Christ as Neither Part nor Whole, Yet Both: Dionysian Paradox

Chapter III, "Cristo non è la parte né il tutto e, nondimeno, è il tutto e la parte," works through Hierotheus's paradox (as transmitted by Dionysius): Jesus is neither part nor whole but comprehends and pre-contains all parts and the whole in himself. Zorzi unpacks this through a series of analogies: a perfect gem in a rotten wooden casket, the sun producing all plants and animals without itself being a plant or animal, a substrate illuminating all substances without contamination (as sunlight shines through mud without being polluted).

The political analogies are notable: Christ orders all principles as an emperor orders his commanders while himself transcending every command, or as the pope orders all bishops while himself being a bishop transcending every episcopate. These analogies reinforce the hierarchical ecclesiology that runs throughout the work.

> «È la forma che crea e induce la forma nelle cose informi, in quanto vero Datore delle forme e il Principe della forma che non coincide con alcuna forma determinata, ma è al di sopra di tutte le forme, come il sole riscalda con il suo favore tutte le erbe e gli animali che devono vivere senza essere un animale o una pianta.»

<cite>p. 1579 (Italian text, lines 261–265)</cite>

---

## Encyclopaedia of Christ's Names: Scripture, Nature, and the Planets

Chapter IV, "Dalle Scritture sappiamo che Cristo è tutte le cose," and Chapter V, "Cristo ha rivendicato per sé i nomi di molte cose artificiali," constitute an extended scriptural and cosmological dossier. Working from lowest to highest — elements, minerals, plants, animals, celestial bodies, artificial constructs, divine names — Zorzi assembles a comprehensive list of scriptural identifications:

- **Elements**: earth (Psalm 85:12), water, air (the breath of John 20:22), fire (Luke 12:49).
- **Plants**: grain of wheat (John 12:24), tree of life and of knowledge, vine.
- **Minerals**: rock/petra (Paul in 1 Cor 10:4), cornerstone (Eph 2:20), gold (Rev 3:18), silver (Ps 12:6).
- **Animals**: lion of Judah (Rev 5:5), calf/ox (Luke 15:23), lamb (John 1:29, with the Baptist's *Ecce Agnus Dei*), eagle (Isaiah 53:5; Deut 32:11 — Moses's canticle).
- **Celestial bodies**: sun of justice (Malachi 4:2), moon (Ps 89:37), *Oriens* (Zechariah 6:12).
- **Planets**: Mercury (the divine interpreter), Venus (love, John 15:12), Mars (judge, Acts 10:42), Jupiter (grace and truth, John 1:17), Saturn and the true Sabbath (Matt 11:28–29).
- **Mountains**: the elevated mountain (Micah 4:1; Ps 68:16), *mons coagulatus*, *mons pinguis*.
- **Angel**: the angel of the covenant in Malachi 3:1.
- **Divine being**: Dionysius's *on* (being as such), equated with Plato's *on* in the *Epistles* and Hierotheus's *supersubstantiale ens*; also the *rex saeculorum* of 1 Tim 1:17.

The Kabbalistic dimension reappears when Zorzi notes that the Hebrew letter *Beth* (ב) is the seal assigned in Hebrew theology to the supreme Wisdom (i.e., the sefirah Chokhmah, identified with the Son), signifying "house" (*domus*): Christ is the great house of God in which all forms were stored before appearing in their proper subjects, a tabernacle "not made by human hands."

The linguistic-philosophical underpinning is explicit: all these names were not assigned by chance but according to the nature of the named, in the primordial language (Hebrew), as Adam named all things in Genesis. Plato's *Cratylus* is cited as a partial, imitative Greek parallel. The Hebrew names contain the deepest properties of reality; Christ, containing all things eminently, merits all these names.

---

## Key Sources in This Chunk

- **Scripture**: Hebrews 12:2; John 1:3, 14; Colossians 1:15–17; Ephesians 1:22–23; Baruch 3:37; Malachi 3:1; Zechariah 6:12; Psalms 85:12, 12:6, 89:37; Deuteronomy 32:11; Isaiah 53:5; 1 Corinthians 10:4; Revelation 3:18, 5:5; John 1:29; Luke 12:49, 15:23; Acts 10:42; John 1:17; Matthew 11:28–29.
- **Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite**: *De divinis nominibus*, citing the *Elementa theologiae* of Hierotheus at length.
- **Origen**: the tripartite donation of Father/Son/Spirit.
- **Plato**: *Parmenides* (the One preceding being); *Epistola* to Dionysius (the *on*); *Cratylus* (the natural correctness of names).
- **Paul and Johannine tradition**: Christological identification formulas throughout.
- **Hebrew Kabbalah**: the ten sefirot ("dieci elementi"), the letter Beth as *domus*, identification of Chokhmah with the Son/Verbum.

---

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chunk sits at the operational summit of De Harmonia Mundi: after the cosmological and speculative books, Zorzi now addresses the *faculty of operating* — how the harmonious universe grounded in Christ's plenitude is the basis for all human and divine action. The encyclopaedic naming of Christ as every creature is not mere allegory but a systematic claim about ontological participation: every being participates in Christ's being, which is why every name correctly applied to a creature can be applied to Christ "in a better sense." This is the Neoplatonic doctrine of *eminence* (the cause contains its effects more perfectly than they contain themselves) fused with Kabbalistic typology and Pauline Christology into what Zorzi presents as a single coherent tradition.
